breaks: The proposed exception modifies "Every repo is public MIT" as a unit, conflating two different invariants. REQ-031 suspends canonical GitHub visibility; it does not suspend the MIT license, convert the project to closed source, or revoke rights already granted.

The claim that the exception is "TEMPORARY" because "REQ-031 already states reversibility" is unsupported. Reversibility supplies no expiry or maximum duration, and reversing canonical visibility cannot recall clones, privatize existing forks, revoke MIT grants, or necessarily restore third-party GitHub fork topology.

Promise §5 remains contradictory: "every repo is public MIT from setup validation onward, so the portfolio exists on GitHub for the whole build" and "forkable forever" are not literally true as future-access promises during a hide. REQ-014 repeats that "every repo is public MIT, so the portfolio already exists on GitHub."

Other absolute public-state statements remain unqualified, including REQ-005 "All projects are public MIT ... no visibility choice," REQ-007 "Project pages are public," REQ-010 "One public page per project" and "the full task tree is public on every project," and REQ-011 "In-progress showcase (public)." A single global normal-state rule is simpler than adding local carve-outs everywhere.

AT-008.27 is not the only affected test. AT-008.03 and AT-008.21 require post-validation visibility to be public; AT-008.14 says the repo "remains publicly readable"; and AT-008.25 requires anonymous cloning and unrelated-user forking. Each needs a no-active-hide precondition.

A blanket exception to REQ-008's bundled "base-permission invariant" could accidentally relax "members have no default repo access" or the owner/platform-automation-only change rule. AT-008.20 must continue to deny unrelated org members access while the hidden repo is private, while the assigned volunteer and NGO retain their explicit permissions.

Saying remediation merely "preserves the authorized hide" permits an implementation that skips hidden repos entirely. Continuous verification must enforce private while an audited hide is active, restore public after reversal, repair an active-hidden repo leaked public, and distinguish an authorized hide from an arbitrary private flip.

The proposal leaves undefined a repo created or validated after a repo-less project was hidden, lifecycle transitions while hidden, participant repo access, and scanning continuity. The active hide must apply whenever the repo exists and survive lifecycle changes until reversed; secret scanning, push protection, explicit participant access, and org base permissions must remain continuously enforced.

A hide is meaningful only prospectively: it removes ai4good-controlled surfaces and prevents new ordinary access through the canonical repository. It cannot provide retroactive secrecy, so the PRD must not imply that prior clones, forks, redistribution rights, or exposed secrets have been withdrawn.
tensions.amended_resolution: Separate licensing from visibility. Every repository remains MIT-licensed; after validation, its normal canonical GitHub visibility is public. The sole exception is an explicit active, audited founder break-glass record under REQ-031, which makes all otherwise-public platform surfaces unavailable and sets the canonical repo's desired visibility to private whenever that repo exists. This is not a private-project option and provides no retroactive confidentiality: existing clones/forks and previously granted MIT rights persist. The visibility verifier continues running against an exact desired-state function—private before validation, public after validation without an active hide, private while a hide is active—and remediates drift in both directions using the active audit record as authority. Base permission none, owner/platform-automation authority, participant permissions, secret scanning, push protection, repo permanence, and licensing remain invariant. The hide persists independently of lifecycle transitions until the founder reverses it; reversal restores each controlled surface to the visibility appropriate for the project's current lifecycle state. Scope AT-008.03/.14/.21/.25 to no active hide, strengthen AT-008.20/.27 for hidden repos and bidirectional remediation, and make AT-031.01/.09 verify scanning and participant repo access during a hide.
tensions.minimal_prd_change: Make four surgical edits: (1) In Promise §2, replace the composite public-MIT sentence with: "Every repo remains MIT-licensed. After setup validation its canonical GitHub visibility is public unless an active, audited founder break-glass hide under REQ-031 requires it to be private. v1 offers no NGO- or volunteer-selectable private/confidential mode. A hide limits future access through ai4good-controlled surfaces and the canonical repo only; it cannot recall clones or forks, revoke rights in copies already obtained, or provide retroactive confidentiality. Throughout this PRD, statements that projects, pages, listings, showcases, task trees, portfolios, or repos are public describe their normal state subject only to this exception." (2) In Promise §5, replace "the portfolio exists on GitHub for the whole build" with "the portfolio is normally visible on GitHub throughout the build; an active REQ-031 hide may interrupt canonical visibility but not MIT licensing or already distributed copies." (3) In REQ-008, replace the visibility portion of the continuous invariant with: "Desired repo visibility is private before validation, public after validation when no active REQ-031 hide exists, and private while an audited hide is active; scheduled verification continuously remediates to that exact state and never infers authorization from private visibility alone. The exception does not relax base permissions, change authority, participant access, secret scanning, or push protection." (4) Append to REQ-031: "The audited hide remains active across lifecycle transitions until founder reversal, applies whenever a repo exists—including one created later—and un-hide restores each controlled surface to its current lifecycle-appropriate visibility. Reversibility refers only to controlled surfaces and canonical-repo visibility, not prior copies, forks, licenses, or secrecy."

overall: T6 requires editorial amendment, not another founder ruling, because d75 already fixes the hide behavior and the remaining work only separates visibility from licensing and makes existing access, scanning, and remediation invariants explicit.
